text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Adam Peaty's 100m breaststroke final was the slowest for 20 years

LA DEFENSE ARENA – In this stadium, you would usually get a robust telling-off for a dive. During theOlympics, it’s how events in this arena start.

It is a miraculous feat of engineering, several in fact, that the £300m La Défense Arena has gone from rugby stadium, to music venue, to swimming pool in the space of a few months.

Only in May, Taylor Swift was here delighting Parisians with herEarth-shatteringEras Tour, before it was then handed over to Paris 2024 organisers who set about turning it into a world-class swimming arena.

Imagine the surprise then when swimmers arrived in Paris, ready to be awestruck by a unique Olympic venue, only to look down and wonder: is this pool big enough?

It is at least definitely long enough – they could not get that wrong – but at 2.2m deep, it is more than 25 per cent shallower than the recommended 3m depth for an elite pool, and not even in line with current minimums.

“World Aquatics rules for the 2.5m pool depth for swimming and water polo events were enacted in January 2023,” a spokesperson for the global governing body toldi.

“When the Olympic Games were awarded to Paris, France, the facility was approved under previous regulations that allowed a pool depth of 2.2 metres.”

The shallower pool does not mean athletes are at risk of scraping the bottom at any point, but they are all aware of the knock-on effect. A shallower pool means that the disruption of a swimmer is amplified, making the water choppier through races and slowing swimmers down.

Adam Peaty, Great Britain’s superstar swimmer who has broken world records 14 times,won 100m breaststroke silver in a time of 59.05. That is more than two seconds outside his best ever mark and it was the slowest Olympic final in the event for 20 years.

The pool is usually a world records party, but not a single one has been broken at La Défense yet.

On average, over the last three Games, the pool produces seven world records per meet and a total of 26 Olympic records. We are exactly a third of the way through and on track for just 15 Olympic records and no WRs.

“The times are definitely a bit strange here, we’ve seen that across a lot of the events,” says Matt Richards, whowon 200m freestyle silver for Great Britain on Mondaybefore successfully reaching the 100m semi-final on Tuesday.

His teammate Jacob Whittle missed out on the top 16 by just 0.07 seconds, struggling in a distinctly choppier outside lane.

“It’s a bit like swimming in the sea but not to that extent, coming out of the turn, a lot of waves,” Whittle said.

“But it’s nothing to a high extreme, it’s like the 0.1 of a second. It’s just something mentally that you have to deal with, so I’m enjoying it and hopefully I can keep doing that this week.”

A tenth of a second is not an insignificant amount of time, whatever teenager Whittle says, and is enough to leave strategists and sports scientists desperately trying to crack the nut of how best to counter the conditions.

”I think it’s just about trying to get on people’s waves a bit more,” says Anna Hopkin, part of Britain’s world record 4x100m mixed relay team in Tokyo.

“As soon as you get too far behind someone, the waves are a bit more choppy in here, so it can feel a bit harder if you’re a bit too far back.

“So [the tactic is] trying to stay with the pack a bit better.

“For me, going out hard is actually probably better, because I’m ahead of the waves and then I can ride the wave coming back.”

But there is a mental challenge as well as a tactical one. Swimmers are conditioned to live and die by the stopwatch, which is spending more time than ever running at these Games.

“In the relay, my split wasn’t great in the heat,” adds Hopkin.

“So I was a bit disheartened after that, but I pulled it back in the final and chopped off quite a lot of time so it’s definitely possible to go quick in this pool.

“You just have to be a bit more careful with tactics and pacing and try not to take the time to heart as much.”

There is no danger of Richards doing that.

“If you’ve got a lane, you got a chance as far as I’m concerned,” he said. “I don’t really mind where I am in the pool, as long as I’ve got water to dive into.

“In the nicest possible way, nobody cares what time you go at the Olympics. They care where you finish. And so I’m not bothered what times I go here. I just care about where I finish in finals.

“There’s a time and a place to go fast times, and there’s a time and place to try and win. And I think this is the kind of meet, where you want to be doing the winning.”

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: Is the Olympic pool too shallow? Why it’s a problem for swimmers at Paris 2024
author: James Gray
description: Adam Peaty's 100m breaststroke final was the slowest for 20 years
